segments.title: Why Corfu mattered in the Ionian Sea
segments.summary: Corfu's value lay in its position between the Adriatic, Ionian, and eastern Mediterranean routes rather than in the island alone.
segments.body: This opening frames Corfu as a maritime interchange and staging point. Its position near the Adriatic entrance made it useful for movement between Italy, the Ionian Sea, and the eastern Mediterranean. The discussion also introduces the island's natural defensibility, while stressing that strategic value depends on where a fleet can go next.

evidence.note: The lecture describes Corfu as a transit and exchange point at the Adriatic and Ionian nexus.

segments.title: Campo Formio and France's Ionian gateway
segments.summary: The Treaty of Campo Formio dismantled Venice, but France retained the Ionian Islands as a route toward the eastern Mediterranean.
segments.body: The video places the French occupation in the long decline of Venetian power rather than treating Venice's fall as a sudden event. Campo Formio transferred much of Venice to Austria while France kept the Ionian Islands. For France, the islands promised access toward the Levant, Egypt, and India while potentially easing the constraints of British sea power.

evidence.note: The speaker explains Venice's decline, the treaty settlement, and France's strategic interest in the Ionian Islands.

segments.title: French rule left Corfu isolated
segments.summary: French administration struggled for money, local support, and manpower before the Russo-Ottoman fleet arrived.
segments.body: The lecture contrasts French revolutionary language with the local demand for a meaningful political voice. Administrative disorder and financial strain weakened the occupation, and the commander at Corfu had only about six hundred troops when ordered to hold against a far larger force. The point is not that a fortress simply fails, but that its defenders had been left without the resources needed to use it well.

evidence.note: The transcript describes French administrative troubles and the small garrison facing the allied expedition.

segments.title: An unlikely Russo-Ottoman naval alliance
segments.summary: Britain helped bring Russia and the Ottoman Empire into a coalition despite their recent wars and mutual distrust.
segments.body: Corfu became a test of coalition warfare between powers that had repeatedly fought each other. The speaker emphasizes British diplomatic and naval mediation, including officers who helped the partners coordinate practical tasks. Their shared objective did not erase their rivalry, but it made a combined expedition possible at a strategically useful moment.

evidence.note: The lecture describes the alliance as an improbable British-supported arrangement and notes Royal Navy help with reconnaissance and coordination.

segments.title: Ushakov's fleet training and aggressive tactics
segments.summary: Admiral Fyodor Ushakov combined close-range aggression with a strong emphasis on training, reserves, and competent officers.
segments.body: This profile follows Ushakov from his Black Sea service to command of the Mediterranean expedition. The video highlights his preference for closing the enemy, concentrating fire, keeping a reserve, and pursuing defeated opponents where the mission allowed. Just as important, he insisted that officers and sailors could perform their duties, making readiness a central part of his command style.

evidence.note: The speaker summarizes Ushakov's career, tactics, use of reserves, and insistence on gunnery and seamanship training.

segments.title: A combined fleet needed practical cooperation
segments.summary: The coalition combined twelve ships of the line and eleven frigates, but troops, supplies, discipline, and personal relations still shaped what it could do.
segments.body: The force was formidable on paper, but the siege depended on more than hull numbers. Ushakov and Ottoman commander Abdülkadir Bey had to secure troops, ammunition, food, and obedience from local authorities while managing different institutions and expectations. Their correspondence illustrates the daily work of coalition command: keeping cooperation functional even when the wider alliance was fragile.

evidence.note: The transcript gives the combined fleet strength and discusses coordination, troop requests, supply shortages, and relations between Ushakov and Abdülkadir Bey.

segments.title: The Venetian fortress system and its burden
segments.summary: Corfu's old and new Venetian fortresses were formidable, but their size demanded more reliable troops and guns than the French garrison could easily provide.
segments.body: The guide tours the Old and New Fortresses and explains why Venice had built such a dense defensive complex after earlier Ottoman sieges. These works protected an important imperial possession, yet the same scale created a manpower problem for later defenders. A large perimeter and hundreds of guns did not automatically produce a secure defence when the garrison was thin and uneven in quality.

evidence.note: The lecture describes the Venetian fortifications, their earlier siege history, and the difficulty of defending their extent with the French force.

segments.title: Vido: the key to Corfu's harbour approaches
segments.summary: The lightly fortified island of Vido controlled access to Corfu and became the decisive first objective for the allies.
segments.body: Vido lay close enough to affect the approaches between the island and Corfu, yet its French defences were limited to troops and a handful of artillery batteries. The video explains why controlling Vido would let an attacker dominate the harbour approaches and bring pressure onto the main works. It is a concise illustration of how a small outlying position can decide a larger siege.

evidence.note: The speaker identifies Vido's position, limited defences, and control over access to Corfu before beginning the siege narrative.

segments.title: The assault on Vido forced Corfu's surrender
segments.summary: After building shore batteries and reinforcing the blockade, the allies bombarded Vido, landed troops, and opened the way to Corfu's capitulation.
segments.body: The siege began with the occupation of nearby positions and the construction of batteries, then tightened as further ships and Ottoman troops arrived. On 28 February 1799, a four-hour bombardment suppressed Vido's batteries before about two thousand troops landed and captured the island. Guns on Vido then helped compel the remaining French positions, and the garrison surrendered on 3 March before a planned assault on the main forts was needed.

evidence.note: The transcript dates the blockade, Vido bombardment and landing, the capture of outworks, and the French surrender.

segments.title: From allied victory to the Ionian Republic
segments.summary: The settlement created a Russo-Ottoman protectorate before later rivalry and British policy reshaped the Ionian Islands again.
segments.body: The victory did not hand Corfu permanently to either coalition partner. Instead, the Septinsular Republic balanced Russian access to the Mediterranean against Ottoman concern over a strong Russian base near Greece. The video then follows the islands through the collapse of that arrangement, a British protectorate after 1815, and eventual transfer to Greece.

evidence.note: The speaker explains the Septinsular Republic, competing Russian and Ottoman aims, the British protectorate, and the islands' later union with Greece.
